title: Santolina macrophylla
intro: Santolina macrophylla, also known as large-leafed cotton lavender or large-leaved gray Santolina, is a perennial shrubby plant in the family Asteraceae, native to the Mediterranean region. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, making it useful in treating various ailments such as wounds, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Santolina macrophylla include flavonoids, sesquiterpenes, and triterpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Santolina macrophylla include teas, infusions, and tinctures, as well as topical creams and ointments used to treat skin conditions and wounds.
taxonomy: Santolina macrophylla is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. It belongs to the genus Santolina, which includes several species of shrubby plants with aromatic foliage. The genus Santolina is further divided into the subfamily Asteroideae. Santolina macrophylla is native to the Mediterranean region, where it grows as a shrubby perennial. This classification is based on its morphological characteristics and phylogenetic analysis.
uses: Santolina macrophylla, also known as large-lavender or giant gray Santolina, has been used in traditional medicine due to its purported anti-inflammatory properties. The plant's leaves and stems are said to contain essential oils, which may contribute to its medicinal benefits. In some cultures, Santolina macrophylla is used to treat digestive issues and skin conditions. However, more research is needed to confirm the plant's efficacy in treating these conditions. Further studies are required to fully understand the medicinal uses of Santolina macrophylla.

constituents: Santolina macrophylla.
